claimed_contribution: The paper shows that inferred warmth and competence are key mechanisms linking seller conspicuousness to consumer responses in buyer-seller relationships.
It demonstrates that communal versus exchange relationship norms moderate how conspicuousness and wealth cues are decoded, so the same cue can hurt or help seller outcomes.

It identifies persuasion knowledge as a boundary condition: suspicion about persuasion attempts inhibits competence-related benefits of conspicuousness and shifts responses toward negative warmth inferences.
context: The studies are set in interpersonal service encounters in which consumers evaluate sellers, primarily a lawyer described in magazine profiles or video interviews. The work focuses on commercial buyer-seller relationships governed by communal versus exchange norms.
design_and_data: The paper reports four experiments with U.S. undergraduate participants (Study 1: N=91; Study 2: N=169; Study 3: N=61; Study 4: N=155). Study 1 used a 2x2 between-subjects design manipulating relationship norm (communal vs exchange prime) and seller conspicuousness (e.g., Ford/Timex vs Cadillac/Rolex) in a lawyer profile; Study 2 manipulated seller wealth and measured individual communal-exchange orientation; Study 3 used professionally produced lawyer interview videos to manipulate norms and conspicuousness; Study 4 added a persuasion-knowledge prime in a 2x2x2 design. Across studies, participants rated warmth, competence, and attitudes or behavioral intentions, and moderated mediation was tested with bootstrapping.
main_findings: Under communal norms, conspicuous (vs nonconspicuous) sellers were inferred as less warm and received lower consumer attitudes or behavioral intentions.
Under exchange norms, conspicuousness increased inferred competence and improved consumer attitudes or behavioral intentions, while warmth was generally not improved.

Wealth cues produced a similar inference pattern to conspicuousness, and when persuasion knowledge was primed, competence benefits disappeared and conspicuousness mainly triggered lower warmth inferences.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper shows that consumers decode seller conspicuousness differently by relationship norm, reducing warmth and responses in communal relationships but increasing competence and responses in exchange relationships unless persuasion knowledge is activated.
relation_to_prior_work: extends social perception and role congruity perspectives into commercial buyer-seller contexts by showing that warmth and competence are differentially weighted under communal versus exchange norms. introduces empirical evidence on decoding conspicuous consumption cues of sellers, an area the authors describe as largely neglected relative to prior signaling-focused work.
research_gap: Prior research had examined conspicuous signaling more than conspicuous decoding, with little empirical work on the inferences observers make from others' conspicuous consumption. The paper argues that virtually no work had examined how consumers decode sellers' conspicuous cues in buyer-seller relationships or how commercial relationship norms shape those inferences.
research_question: What warmth and competence inferences do consumers make when a seller displays conspicuous consumption cues? How do communal versus exchange relationship norms, and consumers' persuasion knowledge, moderate these inferences, and what are the downstream effects on attitudes and behavioral intentions toward the seller?
